name: Chang'e
description: Chang'e, also known as the Moon Goddess, is a prominent figure in Chinese mythology. Her story varies across different versions, but the most common narrative revolves around her husband, the archer Hou Yi. Hou Yi, a skilled archer, was tasked with shooting down nine of the ten suns that were scorching the earth. For his heroic deed, he received an elixir of immortality from the Queen Mother of the West, Xiwangmu. However, Chang'e consumed the elixir herself, either accidentally or intentionally to prevent it from falling into the wrong hands or to escape a life of hardship. As a result, she ascended to the moon, where she became its goddess. Her story is often associated with the Mid-Autumn Festival, a celebration of the harvest moon and family reunions. During this festival, people admire the moon and eat mooncakes, which are traditionally round pastries symbolizing completeness and unity. Chang'e is a symbol of beauty, grace, and the bittersweet nature of separation and longing. She is often depicted with a jade rabbit as her companion on the moon. Her tale explores themes of love, sacrifice, ambition, and the consequences of choices. Chang'e's enduring popularity demonstrates her significance in Chinese culture and her ability to resonate with audiences across generations.
subtitle: The Moon Goddess of Chinese Lore

keyMyths.title: The Elixir of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Hou Yi receives an elixir of immortality from Xiwangmu. Chang'e consumes the elixir, leading to her ascension to the moon and becoming the Moon Goddess.

keyMyths.title: Chang'e's Flight to the Moon
keyMyths.description: Chang'e's consumption of the elixir causes her to float towards the moon. She establishes her residence there, often depicted with a jade rabbit as her companion.

keyMyths.title: The Mid-Autumn Festival
keyMyths.description: The Mid-Autumn Festival is celebrated in honor of Chang'e and the harvest moon. Families gather, admire the moon, and eat mooncakes, symbolizing unity and completeness.

sources.author: Birrell, Anne
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Chinese mythology, including the story of Chang'e.
sources.text: Provides a comprehensive overview of Chinese mythology, including the story of Chang'e.

sources.author: Werner, E. T. C.

sources.text: A classic collection of Chinese myths and legends, featuring various versions of Chang'e's story.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Chang'e is the goddess of the moon, symbolizing tranquility, reflection, and the cyclical nature of life.

associations.name: Immortality Elixir
associations.significance: Represents the pursuit of eternal life and the consequences of choices made in its pursuit.

associations.name: Jade Rabbit
associations.significance: Chang'e's companion on the moon, symbolizing sacrifice, healing, and longevity.

associations.name: Cassia Tree
associations.significance: A symbolic tree on the moon, representing unattainable desires and eternal growth.

associations.name: Regret
associations.significance: Chang'e's story often carries a sense of regret and isolation due to her separation from her husband and earthly life.

associations.name: Mid-Autumn Festival
associations.significance: Chang'e is central to the Mid-Autumn Festival, a celebration of the harvest moon and family reunions.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Chang'e and Selene are lunar deities, associated with the moon and feminine mystique.
Like Chang'e, Kaguya-hime is a lunar princess who returns to the moon, representing ethereal beauty and longing for home.
Mani is the Norse personification of the Moon, guiding its course across the sky, similar to Chang'e's role as a lunar deity.

worshipPractices.name: Moon Gazing
worshipPractices.description: During the Mid-Autumn Festival, families gather to gaze at the full moon, offering mooncakes and other treats to Chang'e as a sign of respect and gratitude. This practice symbolizes family reunion and appreciation for the harvest.

worshipPractices.name: Altar Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Some families maintain small altars dedicated to Chang'e, where they offer incense, fruits, and mooncakes, especially during the Mid-Autumn Festival. These offerings are meant to honor the goddess and seek her blessings for good fortune and family well-being.

cultural.modernLegacy: Chang'e remains a beloved figure in Chinese culture, celebrated during the Mid-Autumn Festival. Her story inspires art, literature, and film, and the Chinese Lunar Exploration Program is named Chang'e in her honor, reflecting her enduring symbolic significance.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: The details of Chang'e's early life vary depending on the version of the myth. In some accounts, she is depicted as a beautiful and virtuous woman married to the legendary archer Hou Yi. Hou Yi was celebrated for shooting down nine of the ten suns that threatened to scorch the earth, saving humanity from certain destruction. As a reward for his heroism, he received an elixir of immortality from the Queen Mother of the West. However, the elixir was only enough for one person. In anticipation of becoming immortal, Hou Yi entrusted the elixir to Chang'e for safekeeping. Her origins are sometimes tied to being a celestial being herself, sent to the mortal realm for a specific purpose, or simply a mortal woman chosen by fate to play a pivotal role in the celestial drama. Regardless of her origins, her beauty and grace are consistently emphasized, making her a compelling figure in Chinese mythology.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: Chang'e's 'quest' is less about physical journeys and more about the internal and consequential choices she makes. The most well-known narrative revolves around the elixir of immortality. While Hou Yi was away, his apprentice, Feng Meng, attempted to steal the elixir. To prevent it from falling into the wrong hands, Chang'e consumed the elixir herself. As a result, she became immortal and floated towards the moon, forever separated from her husband. Another version of the story suggests that Chang'e took the elixir intentionally, either to escape Hou Yi's tyrannical rule or to ascend to a higher spiritual plane. Regardless of her motivations, her act of consuming the elixir and ascending to the moon is her defining moment, symbolizing a journey of transformation and separation. Her 'quest' is thus a complex exploration of ambition, sacrifice, and the consequences of seeking immortality.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Chang'e's legacy is deeply embedded in Chinese culture, primarily through the Mid-Autumn Festival, a celebration dedicated to the full moon and family reunions. During this festival, families gather to admire the moon, eat mooncakes (which often depict Chang'e), and share stories about the Moon Goddess. She symbolizes beauty, grace, and the bittersweet nature of separation. The tale of Chang'e serves as a cautionary reminder about the pursuit of immortality and the importance of cherishing earthly relationships. Furthermore, Chang'e has experienced a resurgence in modern times. The Chinese Lunar Exploration Program is named after her, with the lunar landers and rovers bearing names like Chang'e 1, Chang'e 2, etc. This reflects her enduring cultural significance and her association with the moon, bridging ancient mythology with modern scientific endeavors. Her story continues to inspire artists, writers, and filmmakers, ensuring that her legacy lives on for generations to come.

shortDescription: The Chinese moon goddess. She drank an elixir of immortality and flew to the moon, where she lives with a jade rabbit. Celebrated during the Mid-Autumn Festival.
